past_medical_history: Resection of abdominal para-aortic paraganglioma at age 11
Right adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma at age 19
Childhood history of episodic hypertension, tachycardia, and diaphoresis, resolved after second surgery
Mild traumatic brain injury 6 weeks ago after a fall from a bicycle, managed conservatively
Borderline hypertension noted on recent primary care visit
family_history: Father with history of paraganglioma, treated surgically at age 40
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.occupation: Graphic designer, works at a computer
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his partner in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Regular cyclist, 3-4 times per week until recent injury

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly inattentive on serial sevens with two errors. Language is fluent with occasional word-finding difficulty. Follows three-step commands. Recalls 2/3 objects at 5 minutes. Fund of knowledge and judgment intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Right eye shows mild proptosis. Visual acuity reduced on the right (20/40 OD, 20/20 OS). Right temporal visual field deficit on confrontation testing. No papilledema. Extraocular movements intact. Decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the right V2 and V3 distributions. No facial asymmetry. Hearing decreased to finger rub on the right. Weber lateralizes to the left. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4+/5 deltoid, 4+/5 biceps, 5/5 triceps, 5/5 grip. Left lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Subtle left pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in all extremities. Proprioception intact at the great toes. Vibration sense intact at the ankles. Decreased facial sensation on the right as noted above.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+ with an equivocal plantar response on the left. Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose slightly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently with normal stride length. Mild left arm swing reduction noted. No Romberg sign.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. One witnessed seizure in the emergency department: left arm tonic-clonic activity lasting approximately 60 seconds with preserved awareness, followed by 10 minutes of postictal confusion.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headaches, new left-sided weakness, and a first-time seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Carlos Mendez is a 35-year-old right-handed man with a significant past surgical history including resection of a para-aortic paraganglioma in childhood and a right adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma at age 19, who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed focal seizure. The patient reports that approximately six weeks ago he sustained a mild traumatic brain injury after falling from his bicycle, for which he was evaluated at an outside hospital. He states that a head scan performed at that time revealed an unexpected finding in his skull base, but he did not follow up on it. Over the ensuing four weeks, he has developed progressively worsening headaches that are predominantly right-sided, worst in the early morning, and occasionally wake him from sleep. Acetaminophen has provided only modest relief. His partner reports that over the past two to three weeks, he has become noticeably more forgetful, occasionally repeating himself and missing deadlines at work, which is uncharacteristic for him. Approximately ten days ago, he began experiencing intermittent difficulty with fine motor tasks in his left hand, such as difficulty using his computer mouse and dropping his coffee mug on two occasions. His partner also noted that he has seemed less animated and more irritable than usual. This morning, while at his desk, his partner witnessed his left arm begin to shake rhythmically for approximately one minute, during which he appeared confused and did not respond to questions. He was brought to the emergency department by ambulance. On arrival, he was alert but reported a persistent mild headache and some confusion about the events. He denies any prior seizure history, recent febrile illness, vision changes beyond what he attributes to his old injury, nausea, vomiting, or weight loss. He has not experienced any recurrence of the childhood symptoms of episodic chest pain, palpitations, or diaphoresis. A non-contrast CT scan obtained in the emergency department demonstrated a large right frontotemporal mass with surrounding hypodensity consistent with edema and approximately 9 mm of midline shift to the left, distinct from and more superior to the previously noted skull-base abnormality. He was admitted to the neurology service for further evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha, better organized over the left hemisphere
background.symmetry: Marked asymmetry with attenuation and slowing over the right frontotemporal region
background.reactivity: Posterior dominant rhythm attenuates with eye opening bilaterally

findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with structural lesion or focal cerebral dysfunction in the right frontotemporal region

findings.frequency: Occasional, approximately 2-3 per minute in drowsiness
findings.morphology: Phase-reversing sharp waves with aftergoing slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptogenic potential in peri-tumoral cortex, correlates with clinical seizure

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, does not obscure key findings

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to concern for elevated intracranial pressure

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating continuous right frontotemporal polymorphic delta activity consistent with a structural lesion, accompanied by right frontotemporal sharp waves indicating epileptogenic potential. Background asymmetry with attenuation and slowing over the right hemisphere. Findings are consistent with a right frontotemporal space-occupying lesion with associated cortical irritability and seizure risk.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure.
recommended_actions: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast for further characterization of the structural lesion
Continuous EEG monitoring if further seizures occur
Initiation of antiepileptic therapy given clinical seizure and epileptiform discharges

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontotemporal region, centered in the inferior frontal and superior temporal gyri, extending into the insular cortex and subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense with scattered foci of T1 hyperintensity suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous material
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous mixed signal intensity with central T2 hyperintensity indicating necrosis
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity extending through the right frontal and temporal white matter, corona radiata, and external capsule
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion along the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity; central areas show facilitated diffusion consistent with necrosis
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick irregular ring enhancement with nodular enhancing components along the superior and medial margins

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of the right sylvian fissure and right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle. Approximately 9 mm leftward midline shift of the septum pellucidum. Mild uncal herniation on the right. Sulcal effacement over the right frontotemporal convexity.
findings.borders: Irregular and infiltrative margins with indistinct interface with adjacent brain parenchyma

findings.location: Right frontal and temporal lobe white matter extending into the ipsilateral corona radiata, external capsule, and anterior limb of the internal capsule

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in the edematous regions

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Finger-like projections extending along white matter tracts

findings.location: Right middle cranial fossa, centered at the jugular foramen extending to the petrous apex

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense with internal flow voids (salt-and-pepper appearance)

findings.mass_effect: Mild expansion of the right jugular foramen. No significant intracranial mass effect from this lesion.

volumetrics.lesion_volume: Approximately 68 cm³ including enhancing and necrotic components of the frontotemporal mass
volumetrics.edema_volume: Approximately 95 cm³ of perilesional FLAIR signal abnormality

additional_observations: No additional parenchymal enhancing lesions identified besides the two described above

The skull-base lesion has imaging characteristics most consistent with a paraganglioma (glomus jugulare) given the salt-and-pepper appearance and avid enhancement — this is a separate entity from the frontotemporal mass
Mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes are not expected for this patient's age
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontotemporal mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, extensive surrounding vasogenic edema, and significant midline shift (9 mm) with early right uncal herniation. Imaging characteristics are highly suggestive of a high-grade glial neoplasm, most likely glioblastoma (WHO grade IV). The lesion involves the inferior frontal and superior temporal gyri and extends into the insular cortex. A separate well-circumscribed avidly enhancing skull-base lesion at the right jugular foramen with salt-and-pepper T2 appearance is most consistent with a glomus jugulare paraganglioma, which is a distinct entity from the frontotemporal mass and correlates with the patient's known paraganglioma history. Neurosurgical consultation and advanced imaging are recommended for the frontotemporal mass.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with thick irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, infiltrative margins, extensive perilesional edema disproportionate to mass size, and restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity

Single dominant lesion without known primary malignancy; infiltrative margins argue against metastasis which tends to be more spherical; however, history of paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma raises the possibility of a rare malignant transformation
Would expect smooth thin ring enhancement and central restricted diffusion representing pus; here the restricted diffusion is at the rim, not center; patient is afebrile
Typically homogeneous enhancement in immunocompetent patients; central necrosis is less common; periventricular predilection not seen here

recommended_actions: MR perfusion to assess relative cerebral blood volume in the enhancing component
MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite ratios
Neurosurgical consultation for biopsy or resection planning
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to exclude systemic malignancy
Initiate dexamethasone for peritumoral edema and mass effect
Consider evaluation of the skull-base lesion with dedicated imaging and biochemical workup for catecholamine excess given patient history

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 82 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. No conduction abnormalities. Normal ECG.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No cardiac abnormalities identified. Adequate baseline ECG prior to potential surgical intervention.

labs.interpretation: Laboratory studies are within normal limits. CBC, coagulation parameters, hepatic and renal function panels are all normal, providing an adequate baseline for surgical intervention and potential future chemotherapy. No laboratory evidence of systemic infection or metabolic derangement to account for the seizure. LDH and CRP are within normal limits.

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right frontotemporal mass
Central necrotic core of the mass

additional_observations: Hot spot of rCBV in the superior-medial enhancing rim, suggesting the highest-grade component and optimal biopsy target
Perilesional edema shows mildly elevated rCBV compared to contralateral normal-appearing white matter, suggesting infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 4.1) in the enhancing rim of the right frontotemporal mass, strongly supporting a high-grade glial neoplasm. The central necrotic core shows expected low perfusion. Perilesional perfusion abnormalities suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the contrast-enhancing boundary. These findings are most consistent with glioblastoma (WHO grade IV).
recommended_actions: Stereotactic biopsy or maximal safe resection
Target superior-medial enhancing rim for biopsy (highest rCBV area) if biopsy rather than resection is planned

findings.type: Abnormal metabolite ratios consistent with high-grade neoplasm
findings.location: Enhancing component of right frontotemporal mass

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

findings.location: FLAIR-hyperintense region adjacent to enhancing mass

additional_observations: Spectroscopic pattern in the enhancing mass is characteristic of a high-grade glioma with elevated choline (high membrane turnover), reduced NAA (neuronal loss), and lipid/lactate peaks (necrosis and anaerobic glycolysis)
Perilesional spectral abnormalities suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates a metabolite pattern highly characteristic of a high-grade glial neoplasm: markedly elevated choline reflecting high cellular proliferation, markedly reduced NAA indicating neuronal destruction, and prominent lipid/lactate peaks suggesting necrosis. Perilesional spectral changes suggest infiltrative tumor extending beyond the contrast-enhancing boundary.
recommended_actions: Tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection
Molecular profiling including IDH mutation status, MGMT promoter methylation, and 1p/19q codeletion

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.clinical_significance: No evidence of primary lung malignancy

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Left adrenal normal. Right adrenal absent consistent with prior adrenalectomy.
CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.clinical_significance: Surgical absence of right adrenal gland consistent with prior history of pheochromocytoma resection

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: No recurrent para-aortic mass. No lymphadenopathy.
CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.clinical_significance: No evidence of recurrent paraganglioma at prior surgical site

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions
CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.clinical_significance: No evidence of osseous metastatic disease

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast demonstrates no evidence of a primary malignancy or metastatic disease. The right adrenal gland is surgically absent, consistent with the patient's history of prior adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma. No recurrent para-aortic mass is identified. These findings support a primary brain tumor rather than cerebral metastasis.

Histopathology.value: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (15 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and extensive palisading necrosis
Histopathology.clinical_significance: Histologic features diagnostic of glioblastoma, WHO grade IV
Confirms glial origin of the neoplasm
High proliferative index consistent with aggressive high-grade neoplasm
Argues against neuroendocrine tumor or paraganglioma metastasis; confirms this is a primary glial neoplasm

Molecular_Testing.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with worse prognosis compared to IDH-mutant tumors
Methylated MGMT promoter is associated with improved response to temozolomide chemotherapy
Absence of 1p/19q codeletion excludes oligodendroglioma; consistent with astrocytic lineage
TERT promoter mutation in the setting of IDH-wildtype confirms glioblastoma per WHO 2021 classification
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; may be relevant for targeted therapy trials
Combined gain of chromosome 7 and loss of chromosome 10 is a hallmark of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms the diagnosis of glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV (CNS WHO 2021). Histopathology demonstrates all cardinal features including microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis. Chromogranin A is negative, confirming this is a primary glial tumor and not a metastasis from the patient's prior paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma history. Molecular testing reveals IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation and EGFR amplification, confirming the integrated diagnosis. MGMT promoter is methylated, which is associated with improved response to temozolomide. 1p/19q codeletion is absent, excluding oligodendroglioma.
abnormal_values_summary: Microscopic description: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis — diagnostic of glioblastoma WHO grade IV
Ki-67 proliferation index: 40% (ref < 3%) — high proliferative index consistent with aggressive neoplasm
IDH1/IDH2 mutation: Wild-type — associated with worse prognosis
MGMT promoter methylation: Methylated — favorable for temozolomide response
TERT promoter mutation: Present — confirms glioblastoma per WHO 2021
EGFR amplification: Present — common in IDH-wildtype GBM
+7/-10 chromosome: Present — hallmark of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma

signal_characteristics.left_hand_activation: BOLD activation identified in the right precentral gyrus, displaced superiorly and posteriorly by the mass. The activation focus for left hand motor function is approximately 12 mm from the superior margin of the tumor.
signal_characteristics.right_hand_activation: Normal BOLD activation in the left precentral gyrus, anatomically intact
signal_characteristics.foot_activation: Bilateral paracentral lobule activation, normal position

findings.mass_effect: The tumor displaces the right primary motor cortex superiorly and posteriorly. Hand knob region is distorted but identifiable.

signal_characteristics.Broca_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus
signal_characteristics.Wernicke_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus

additional_observations: DTI tractography demonstrates displacement of the right corticospinal tract superiorly and posteriorly by the mass; the tract appears intact but stretched
The uncinate fasciculus on the right is partially disrupted by the tumor
No significant BOLD activation within the enhancing mass itself
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that the right frontotemporal mass displaces the primary motor cortex superiorly and posteriorly. The BOLD activation for left hand motor function is approximately 12 mm from the tumor margin, providing a modest buffer zone. The patient is left-hemisphere dominant for language, and language areas are not at direct surgical risk. DTI tractography shows displacement but apparent preservation of the right corticospinal tract. These findings should be integrated into neuronavigation planning for maximal safe resection.
recommended_actions: Integrate fMRI and DTI data into neuronavigation system for surgical planning
Consider awake craniotomy with intraoperative motor mapping
Use 5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery to maximize extent of resection

output.query: glioblastoma standard treatment protocol Stupp regimen young adult

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: Concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide with radiotherapy significantly improved median survival (14.6 vs 12.1 months) and 2-year survival (26.5% vs 10.4%) compared to radiotherapy alone

results.title: Effect of tumor-treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide vs maintenance temozolomide alone on survival in patients with glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of tumor-treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival (20.9 vs 16.0 months) in newly diagnosed glioblastoma

results.title: MGMT gene silencing and benefit from temozolomide in glioblastoma
results.authors: Hegi ME, Diserens AC, Gorlia T, et al.
results.key_finding: MGMT promoter methylation is a strong predictive biomarker for benefit from temozolomide; methylated patients show significantly better overall survival with temozolomide

results.title: Glioblastoma in young adults: a distinct molecular and clinical entity
results.key_finding: GBM in patients under 40 is uncommon but aggressive; IDH-wildtype tumors in younger adults carry a prognosis intermediate between IDH-mutant and older IDH-wildtype patients; maximal safe resection and Stupp protocol remain standard of care

output.summary: The current standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma is maximal safe surgical resection followed by the Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m²), followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m², days 1-5 of 28-day cycle). Tumor-treating fields (TTFields/Optune) are recommended as an addition to maintenance temozolomide. MGMT promoter methylation status is an important prognostic and predictive biomarker; methylated patients derive greater benefit from temozolomide. In younger patients with GBM, the treatment approach remains the same, though prognosis may be slightly better than in elderly patients.

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose then 4 mg IV every 6 hours; Levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily
interactions: Amlodipine + Dexamethasone: Corticosteroids may reduce antihypertensive effect; monitor blood pressure closely
warnings: Dexamethasone may cause steroid-induced hyperglycemia; monitor blood glucose regularly even in non-diabetic patients
Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects including irritability and mood changes; monitor given patient's partner-reported irritability
Consider GI prophylaxis with proton pump inhibitor while on high-dose corticosteroids
Taper dexamethasone to lowest effective dose as soon as clinically feasible
output.formulary_status: Both medications on formulary, no restrictions
alternatives: Methylprednisolone as alternative corticosteroid, though dexamethasone is preferred in neuro-oncology
Lacosamide as alternative antiepileptic if levetiracetam causes intolerable behavioral side effects

Catecholamine_Panel.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of functioning pheochromocytoma recurrence
Normal, no biochemical evidence of catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma activity from the skull-base lesion
Normal; unlike the source case, this skull-base lesion does not appear to be functionally active
Normal, no elevation to suggest active neuroendocrine tumor burden

output.interpretation: Catecholamine workup is entirely normal. Plasma free metanephrines and normetanephrines, 24-hour urine catecholamine metabolites, and chromogranin A are all within reference ranges. Despite the patient's history of paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma and the incidental skull-base lesion with imaging features of a glomus jugulare paraganglioma, there is no biochemical evidence of catecholamine hypersecretion. The skull-base lesion is likely non-functioning. This workup does not change the management of the primary frontotemporal mass, which requires tissue diagnosis.

differential.key_features: Patient has history of paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma; however, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no recurrence, the frontotemporal mass has infiltrative margins more typical of a primary glial tumor, and chromogranin A immunostaining is negative in the biopsy specimen
No fever, normal WBC, no recent infection; restricted diffusion is at the enhancing rim (high cellularity) rather than centrally (pus); thick irregular ring enhancement argues against abscess
Immunocompetent young patient; central necrosis and heterogeneous enhancement atypical for lymphoma in immunocompetent patients; not periventricular
No history of multiple sclerosis; closed ring enhancement and significant mass effect with midline shift argue against demyelination; extensive necrosis not characteristic
Low-grade gliomas are typically non-enhancing, slower growing, and lack necrosis; this mass has all features of a high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent CT head to evaluate for mass lesion, assess midline shift, and herniation risk
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large right frontotemporal hyperdense mass with surrounding edema and midline shift

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose followed by 4 mg IV every 6 hours to reduce peritumoral edema and mass effect
Start levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily for seizure management given presenting focal seizure
Order MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast to characterize the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, surrounding edema, midline shift, and an incidental skull-base lesion consistent with paraganglioma

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and guide antiepileptic management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontotemporal focal slowing with epileptiform discharges in peri-tumoral cortex

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and liver function tests
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal baseline labs suitable for surgical planning and future chemotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Request MR perfusion imaging to assess tumor vascularity and grade
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim consistent with high-grade tumor

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out primary or recurrent malignancy elsewhere
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of extracranial malignancy or recurrent paraganglioma

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for stereotactic biopsy or maximal safe resection of the frontotemporal mass
Request functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced by tumor, language lateralized to left hemisphere

optimal_actions.action: Following tissue diagnosis confirming GBM, plan Stupp protocol: concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy + temozolomide) followed by adjuvant temozolomide
Evaluate the incidental skull-base paraganglioma separately with catecholamine workup and otolaryngology/skull base surgery consultation

critical_actions: Initiate dexamethasone to reduce peritumoral edema and prevent herniation given 9 mm midline shift
Start antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam) given presenting focal seizure
Obtain MRI brain with contrast to characterize the mass
Rule out metastatic disease with systemic imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis), especially given paraganglioma history
Obtain tissue diagnosis via neurosurgical biopsy or resection
Correctly identify the frontotemporal mass as the primary pathology requiring urgent treatment, distinct from the incidental skull-base lesion
Plan for Stupp protocol (concurrent chemoradiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide) after tissue diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass with significant midline shift and early uncal herniation (herniation risk)
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis
Delaying corticosteroids in a patient with significant mass effect and midline shift
Attributing the frontotemporal mass to paraganglioma metastasis without tissue confirmation and initiating inappropriate treatment
Starting temozolomide prior to tissue confirmation of diagnosis
Administering anticoagulation without clear indication given upcoming surgical planning
key_reasoning_points: The combination of progressive headaches worst in the morning, new focal neurological deficits (left arm weakness), and new-onset seizure in a young adult is a classic presentation of a brain tumor requiring urgent imaging
CT showing a large mass with midline shift mandates immediate dexamethasone for cerebral edema and neurosurgical consultation
MRI features of heterogeneous ring enhancement with central necrosis, thick irregular rim, and extensive perilesional edema are characteristic of glioblastoma even in a young patient

The patient's history of paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma and the incidental skull-base lesion raise the differential of metastatic neuroendocrine tumor, but the imaging features of the frontotemporal mass are more consistent with a primary glial neoplasm
The skull-base lesion with salt-and-pepper T2 appearance and avid enhancement is a separate entity most consistent with a glomus jugulare paraganglioma and should be evaluated independently
Metastasis workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) is essential, particularly given the history of prior catecholamine-secreting tumors
Tissue diagnosis is critical before initiating therapy; molecular markers (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) guide prognosis and treatment decisions
Chromogranin A negativity on biopsy confirms the frontotemporal mass is a primary glial tumor, not a paraganglioma metastasis

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward GBM presentation with classic symptoms (progressive headaches, focal deficits, seizure) and characteristic MRI findings. The patient's prior paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma history and incidental skull-base lesion add clinical interest but do not significantly obscure the primary diagnosis, as the frontotemporal mass has clear high-grade glioma imaging features.
